content:
**Reproduction** — biological process by which living organisms produce **offspring** of their own kind, ensuring **species continuity**.

**Asexual reproduction** — new individuals from **one parent** only; **no fusion of gametes**; offspring are **genetically identical** (clones); rapid, no need to find a mate.

**Binary fission** (unicellular):
• Parent cell divides into **two equal daughter cells** (e.g. **Amoeba**, bacteria).
• Nucleus divides first (mitosis), then cytoplasm splits.

**Multiple fission** — nucleus divides repeatedly, then many daughter cells released (e.g. **Plasmodium**).

**Budding** — small **outgrowth (bud)** on parent body develops into new individual, may detach or remain colonial (e.g. **Hydra**, **yeast**).
• Bud receives nucleus, grows, eventually separates when mature.

**Must know**
• Asexual = one parent, no gamete fusion, identical offspring.
• Fission in Amoeba = binary; yeast/Hydra = budding.
• Asexual is fast and preserves well-adapted genotypes but no variation.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Clone — genetically identical copy of parent.
• Bud — undeveloped outgrowth that can form a new organism.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Asexual vs sexual: one parent vs two; no variation vs variation; faster vs slower.
• 'Budding involves meiosis' is FALSE — mitotic divisions only.
• Yeast budding leaves a **bud scar** on parent cell.
teacherTip: Budding = Baby outgrowth; Fission = split into two — picture Hydra with bump, Amoeba pinching in half.
examTip: Name the organism with the method: Amoeba–fission, Hydra–budding, yeast–budding — match-column staple.

content:
**Fragmentation** — parent body breaks into pieces; each fragment grows into a complete new organism (e.g. **Spirogyra** alga filaments, **Planaria** flatworm).

**Spore formation** — specialised **reproductive cells (spores)** produced in large numbers inside **sporangia**; light, walled, dispersed by wind/water; germinate in suitable conditions (e.g. **Rhizopus** bread mould, **ferns**, mosses).
• Spores are **not seeds** — unicellular, no stored food reserve like endosperm.

**Vegetative propagation** — new plants from **vegetative parts** (root, stem, leaf) without seeds or flowers — still asexual.
• **Stem**: runners (strawberry), stolons, tubers (potato — eyes are buds), bulbs (onion), rhizomes (ginger).
• **Root**: sweet potato (adventitious buds on roots), dahlias.
• **Leaf**: **Bryophyllum** — buds (plantlets) on leaf margins.

**Must know**
• Fragmentation needs no special reproductive organ — just breakage + regeneration.
• Spores = asexual in fungi/ferns; produced in millions for dispersal.
• Vegetative propagation = artificial or natural cloning of crop plants.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Sporangium — structure producing spores.
• Vegetative propagation — asexual reproduction using non-reproductive plant parts.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Spore (single cell, no embryo) ≠ seed (embryo + food + coat).
• Potato tuber is modified stem, not root — 'eyes' are axillary buds.
• Bryophyllum plantlets are vegetative, not from flowers.
teacherTip: Bryophyllum = Babies on leaf margin; Potato eyes = buds on stem tuber — don't call tuber a root.
examTip: Give two examples each of natural vegetative propagation from stem and leaf — strawberry runner + potato tuber; Bryophyllum + Begonia leaf.

content:
**Artificial vegetative propagation** — horticultural methods to grow desirable plants quickly with known traits.

**Grafting:**
• Join **scion** (shoot of desired variety) to **stock/rootstock** (root system of hardy variety).
• Vascular cambium of both must align and fuse; tied + sealed until union forms.
• Examples: mango, rose, citrus, apple.
• Stock provides roots/disease resistance; scion provides fruit/flower quality.

**Layering** — stem still attached to parent while roots form at bent buried portion (e.g. jasmine, grape, bougainvillea).

**Cutting** — stem/twig planted in moist soil develops adventitious roots (e.g. rose, sugarcane, money plant).

**Tissue culture (micropropagation):**
• Small **explant** (meristem/cell) grown on sterile nutrient medium → **callus** → many plantlets (clones) in weeks.
• Disease-free, rapid mass production (orchids, banana, potato).

**Must know**
• Grafting combines two plants; cutting/layering uses one plant.
• Scion = upper/desired; Stock = lower/root part.
• Tissue culture needs sterile lab conditions and plant hormones (auxin, cytokinin).
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Scion — grafted shoot bearing desired traits.
• Stock — rooted base providing support and nutrients.
• Explant — small plant tissue used to initiate culture.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Grafted plant is genetically chimeric — fruit from scion tissue, roots from stock.
• 'Grafting works between any two species' is FALSE — close relatives needed for vascular union.
• Tissue culture is asexual — all plantlets identical.
teacherTip: Scion on top = Superior fruit; Stock below = Strong roots — S-S mnemonic.
examTip: Label scion and stock in graft diagram; state why cambium contact is essential — one mark each.

content:
A **flower** is the **reproductive organ** of angiosperms (flowering plants). Typical parts arranged as **four whorls** on **thalamus/receptacle**:

**Calyx (outermost)** — sepals; usually green; protect flower bud.

**Corolla** — petals; often coloured/scented; attract pollinators.

**Androecium (male whorl)** — stamens; each stamen = **filament** + **anther**; anther produces **pollen grains** (male gametophytes / contain male gametes).

**Gynoecium / pistil (female whorl)** — usually one or more carpels; each carpel has **stigma** (sticky landing platform) + **style** (stalk) + **ovary** (base, contains **ovules** with egg cell / female gamete).

**Complete flower** — all four whorls present (e.g. mustard, china rose).
**Incomplete flower** — one or more whorls missing (e.g. corn — no petals).
**Bisexual (hermaphrodite)** — both androecium and gynoecium (e.g. hibiscus).
**Unisexual** — staminate (male) or pistillate (female) only (e.g. papaya, watermelon).

**Must know**
• Stamen = male; carpel/pistil = female.
• Ovule inside ovary → after fertilisation becomes seed; ovary wall → fruit.
• Pollen lands on stigma, not ovary directly.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Pollen grain — male reproductive unit produced in anther.
• Ovule — structure in ovary containing female gamete (egg).

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Petal attracts; sepal protects — don't swap functions.
• 'Ovary becomes seed' is FALSE — ovule → seed, ovary → fruit.
• Essential whorls = androecium + gynoecium (for reproduction); calyx + corolla = non-essential (accessory).
teacherTip: From outside in: Calyx, Corolla, Androecium, Gynoecium — CCAG or 'Can Cats Attack Gerbils' for whorl order.
examTip: Draw longitudinal section of bisexual flower labelling all parts — ICSE diagram question almost every cycle.

content:
**Pollination** — transfer of **pollen grains** from **anther** to **stigma** of a flower. Essential BEFORE fertilisation can occur.

**Self-pollination** — pollen from anther lands on stigma of the **same flower** or **another flower on same plant** (e.g. pea, wheat, rice).
• Economical (little pollen wasted); no pollinator needed; less genetic variation.

**Cross-pollination** — pollen transferred to stigma of a flower on a **different plant of same species** (e.g. mustard, sunflower, apple).
• More genetic variation; needs agents; pollen produced in large quantities.

**Pollinating agents:**
• **Insects** (entomophily) — bright petals, scent, nectar; sticky/rough pollen; sticky stigma (e.g. rose, jasmine).
• **Wind** (anemophily) — small dull flowers, no nectar; pollen light, smooth, abundant; long hanging anthers; feathery stigma (e.g. grass, maize, pine).
• **Water** (hydrophily) — rare (e.g. Vallisneria).

**Must know**
• Pollination ≠ fertilisation — pollination is pollen transfer; fertilisation is fusion of gametes inside ovule.
• Insect-pollinated: large colourful flowers; wind-pollinated: inconspicuous, pollen wastage high.
• Cross-pollination increases adaptability of species.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Entomophily — insect pollination.
• Anemophily — wind pollination.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Self-pollination: small pollen number, short filaments; Cross/wind: huge pollen, long exposed anthers.
• 'Pollen reaches ovule directly' is FALSE — must germinate on stigma, grow pollen tube through style.
• Maize tassels (male) and silks (female stigma) — classic wind pollination example.
teacherTip: Wind = Wasteful pollen, feathery stigma; Insect = Inviting colours, nectar, sticky pollen.
examTip: Tabular compare self vs cross pollination — examiners love 4 differences (agents, pollen number, variation, flower type).

content:
**Fertilisation (double fertilisation in angiosperms — foundation level):**
• Pollen grain germinates on stigma → **pollen tube** grows down style into ovary.
• Male gamete fuses with **egg cell** in ovule → **zygote** (diploid) → develops into **embryo**.
• (In advanced texts: second male gamete fuses with polar nuclei → endosperm — triploid food tissue.)

**Post-fertilisation changes:**
• **Ovule** → **seed** (contains embryo + stored food + seed coat/testa).
• **Ovary wall** → **fruit** (pericarp); protects seeds, aids dispersal.
• Other floral parts (petals, stamens, style, stigma) usually **wither and fall**.
• **Ovary** alone ripens into fruit; other whorls do not become fruit.

**Fruit** — ripened ovary; may be fleshy (mango, tomato) or dry (mustard, pea pod).
**True fruit** — develops only from ovary (apple flesh is thalamus — false fruit accessory).

**Must know**
• Fertilisation occurs inside ovule, not on stigma.
• Zygote → embryo; ovule → seed; ovary → fruit.
• Seed dispersal follows fruit/seed maturation.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Zygote — diploid cell formed by fusion of male and female gametes.
• Embryo — young plant in seed with radicle, plumule, cotyledons.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Pollination (external transfer) happens before fertilisation (internal fusion).
• 'Petal becomes fruit' is FALSE.
• Tomato is a fruit botanically (develops from ovary) though used as vegetable in cooking.
teacherTip: O-S-F chain: Ovule → Seed, Ovary → Fruit — write this chain first in every post-fertilisation answer.
examTip: Label pollen tube path: stigma → style → ovary → micropyle → synergids/egg — diagram-based MCQs common in Olympiads.

content:
A **seed** is a mature **ovule** containing an **embryo** ready for dispersal and germination.

**Parts of a typical dicot seed (e.g. bean):**
• **Seed coat (testa)** — protective outer layer; **micropyle** — small pore for water entry and oxygen.
• **Cotyledons** — seed leaves; store food (thick in bean) or absorb endosperm (wheat has one cotyledon).
• **Embryo** — **radicle** (future root), **plumule** (future shoot), **hypocotyl**.

**Monocot seed (e.g. maize/corn):**
• Single cotyledon (scutellum); food stored in **endosperm**; embryo small.

**Germination conditions:** water (activates enzymes), oxygen (respiration), suitable temperature; light needed only for some seeds.

**Types:**
• **Epigeal** — cotyledons pushed above ground (bean).
• **Hypogeal** — cotyledons remain underground (pea, maize).

**Must know**
• Seed = ovule after fertilisation; dispersal avoids competition with parent.
• Micropyle allows water uptake for imbibition.
• Cotyledons provide food until seedling can photosynthesise.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Germination — resumption of growth of embryo into seedling.
• Imbibition — absorption of water causing seed to swell.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Dicot = two cotyledons; Monocot = one cotyledon.
• 'Seed and fruit develop simultaneously' — fruit often encloses seed.
• Dormancy — seed waits for favourable season before germinating.
teacherTip: Radicle Roots first, Plumule Pushes shoot — R-before-P in germination order.
examTip: Identify parts in soaked bean seed dissection — radicle, plumule, cotyledon, testa, micropyle labels expected.

content:
**Seed dispersal** — scattering of seeds away from parent plant to reduce competition for light, water, minerals and space; colonise new habitats.

**By wind (anemochory):**
• Light seeds, winged (maple, drumstick/Moringa), hairy parachutes (dandelion, cotton, calotropis).

**By water (hydrochory):**
• Fibrous husk, air spaces — coconut floats on sea currents for long distances.

**By animals (zoochory):**
• Hooks/spines stick to fur (Xanthium, burdock); edible fruits — seeds pass through gut unharmed (tomato, guava) or dropped after eating.

**By explosive mechanism (autochory):**
• Sudden drying causes pod to split/shatter flinging seeds (balsam 'touch-me-not', pea, castor).

**By humans/agencies:**
• Agriculture, trade, accidental transport.

**Must know**
• Each adaptation matches dispersal agent (wing → wind, hook → animal fur).
• Dispersal increases survival and species spread.
• Fruit aids animal dispersal by being colourful, tasty, nutritious.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Dispersal — transport of seeds/ fruits away from parent plant.
• Autochory — self-dispersal by explosive fruits.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Coconut = water dispersal, not wind (heavy with fibrous coat for floating).
• Cotton fibres aid wind dispersal of seeds.
• 'All fruits are dispersed by animals' is FALSE.
examTip: Match seed/fruit to agent: maple–wind, coconut–water, Xanthium–animal, balsam–explosion — pure recall MCQs.

subtopic: 9. Sexual vs Asexual Reproduction in Plants
content:
**Sexual reproduction in plants** — involves **formation of gametes**, **pollination**, **fertilisation**; offspring show **genetic variation**; slower but enables evolution and adaptation.

**Asexual reproduction** — no gametes, no fertilisation; **one parent**; **identical** offspring; rapid, used for stable crop varieties.

| Feature | Asexual | Sexual |
| Mode | Vegetative parts, spores, fission, budding | Flowers, gametes, seeds |
| Parents | One | Two (male + female gametes) |
| Variation | None (clones) | Present |
| Speed | Fast | Slower (needs pollination + seed) |
| Examples | Potato, rose cutting, fern spores | Mustard, mango (from seed), pea |

**When each is advantageous:**
• Asexual — preserve hybrid quality, rapid orchard expansion, no pollinator needed.
• Sexual — new combinations of genes, seed dormancy survives harsh seasons, long-distance dispersal.

**Must know**
• Higher plants reproduce both ways; farmers use asexual for clones, sexual for new varieties.
• Variation from sexual reproduction is raw material for natural selection.
• Hybrid seeds (sexual) may not breed true in next generation.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Gamete — haploid reproductive cell (pollen/egg in plants).
• Hybrid — offspring from parents of different varieties/species with mixed traits.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• 'Asexual reproduction involves mitosis only' — TRUE for cell division aspect.
• 'Sexual reproduction always needs flowers' — TRUE for angiosperms; ferns use spores + fertilisation (alternation of generations).
• Seed formation requires fertilisation; tuber sprouting does not.
teacherTip: Sexual = Shuffle genes (variation); Asexual = Xerox copy (identical).
examTip: Give two advantages of each type — standard 4-mark question; mention variation for sexual, speed and clone fidelity for asexual.

subtopic: 10. Complete Reproductive Cycle — Flower to New Plant
content:
**Life cycle summary (angiosperm):**

1. **Flower develops** on mature plant.
2. **Pollen** forms in anther; **ovule** with egg forms in ovary.
3. **Pollination** — pollen to stigma (self or cross).
4. **Pollen tube** carries male gamete to ovule.
5. **Fertilisation** — zygote forms → **embryo**; ovule → **seed**; ovary → **fruit**.
6. **Seed dispersal** by wind/water/animals/explosion.
7. **Germination** under suitable conditions — radicle → root, plumule → shoot.
8. Seedling grows into mature flowering plant → cycle repeats.

**Alternation in farmers' practice:**
• Mango grafting (asexual) for known sweet variety.
• Mustard/wheat from seed (sexual) each season.
• Banana — sterile seeds, propagated vegetatively (suckers).

**Must know**
• Correct sequence: pollination BEFORE fertilisation; fertilisation BEFORE seed/fruit formation.
• Fruit protects and helps disperse seeds.
• Full cycle links reproduction to species survival and spread.
explanation:
**Definitions:**
• Life cycle — series of stages from one generation to the next.
• Seedling — young plant after germination.

**Compare / Exam traps:**
• Order trap: fertilisation cannot occur before pollination.
• 'Fruit formation without fertilisation' — parthenocarpic fruits (banana, some oranges) possible but no true seeds.
• Dispersal is NOT the same as germination.
teacherTip: Write the 7-step chain on flashcard: Flower → Pollen/Ovule → Pollination → Pollen tube → Fertilisation → Seed/Fruit → Dispersal → Germination.
examTip: Arrange jumbled stages questions — number pollination as step before fertilisation; seed formation after fertilisation only.
